choices.prompt: In a blue-green deployment as the module describes it, what is the single biggest advantage of keeping the idle 'blue' environment running after you flip all traffic to 'green'?
choices: Rollback is near-instant: if green misbehaves you flip back to blue, which is still running the old version
It lets you expose the new version to only 5% of real traffic at first while you watch error rates
It avoids running two full environments, cutting infrastructure cost in half
It guarantees that bugs missed by smoke tests will never reach any user
correct_answers: Rollback is near-instant: if green misbehaves you flip back to blue, which is still running the old version

choices.prompt: The module contrasts canary and ring deployments. Which statement best captures how ring deployment differs from canary?
choices: Ring deployment generalizes canary to audiences (ring 0 internal staff, ring 1 opt-in early adopters, ring 2 everyone) rather than just a raw percentage of traffic
Ring deployment flips all traffic at once between two identical environments, while canary moves gradually
Ring deployment requires no telemetry, while canary cannot work without watching error rate and latency
Ring deployment is a routing change between a slot and production, while canary redeploys the app
correct_answers: Ring deployment generalizes canary to audiences (ring 0 internal staff, ring 1 opt-in early adopters, ring 2 everyone) rather than just a raw percentage of traffic

choices.prompt: According to the module, what specifically makes a deployment slot swap a low-downtime technique and 'a lightweight blue-green'?
choices: The new code is fully started and warmed in the slot before the swap, so the swap is just a routing change to already-warm instances rather than a cold start
The swap automatically rewrites the database schema so old and new versions never conflict
The swap deploys the new build fresh into production at the moment of cutover, ensuring the latest code
The swap exposes the new version to only a small percentage of users while monitoring latency
correct_answers: The new code is fully started and warmed in the slot before the swap, so the swap is just a routing change to already-warm instances rather than a cold start

choices.prompt: Select all statements that the module makes about feature flags and Azure App Configuration's Feature Manager.
choices: Deploying code and releasing a feature are different events: you can ship code disabled behind a flag and turn it on later without another deployment
Feature Manager centralizes flags outside your code and can target audiences such as a percentage of users or a named group
Flags should be treated as temporary and removed once a feature is fully rolled out and stable, to avoid permanent technical debt
Turning a feature off requires a fresh redeployment of the application to production
correct_answers: Deploying code and releasing a feature are different events: you can ship code disabled behind a flag and turn it on later without another deployment
Feature Manager centralizes flags outside your code and can target audiences such as a percentage of users or a named group
Flags should be treated as temporary and removed once a feature is fully rolled out and stable, to avoid permanent technical debt

choices.prompt: During a rolling deployment, a small percentage of requests start failing intermittently and the errors disappear once the rollout completes. Per the module, what does this most likely indicate, and what is the prescribed prevention?
choices: A backward-compatibility problem in the mixed-version window (e.g. a non-additive schema change the old version cannot read); make schema changes additive and deploy them ahead of the code that requires them
A cold-start problem; warm the slot fully before performing the swap
A canary measured without telemetry; define in advance what 'unhealthy' means and watch error rate and latency
A permanent feature flag left in the code; remove the dead branch once the feature is stable
correct_answers: A backward-compatibility problem in the mixed-version window (e.g. a non-additive schema change the old version cannot read); make schema changes additive and deploy them ahead of the code that requires them

choices.prompt: The module describes how a rolling deployment updates instances behind a load balancer. Which statement correctly captures its mechanics and its primary cost?
choices: Instances are updated in batches — taken out of rotation, updated, returned — so capacity stays up with no full outage, at the cost of a window where both the old and new versions run at once
All instances are updated simultaneously to guarantee every request hits the new version, at the cost of a brief full outage
A single idle clone of production is warmed and then traffic flips to it all at once, at the cost of running two full environments
Traffic is routed to only 5% of instances first and widened gradually, at the cost of requiring detailed telemetry to judge health
correct_answers: Instances are updated in batches — taken out of rotation, updated, returned — so capacity stays up with no full outage, at the cost of a window where both the old and new versions run at once

choices.prompt: Per the module's account of how a canary release progresses, what does the team do at each stage to decide whether to continue?
choices: Release to a small percentage (e.g. 5%) first and watch error rates and latency; if it stays healthy, widen to 25% then 100%, and if it degrades, halt and roll back
Deploy to the idle environment, smoke-test it, then flip 100% of traffic at once and watch for rollback signals
Promote audience by audience — ring 0 internal staff, then ring 1 early adopters, then ring 2 everyone — without measuring traffic percentages
Swap a warmed slot into production and rely on the swap-back as the sole health gate
correct_answers: Release to a small percentage (e.g. 5%) first and watch error rates and latency; if it stays healthy, widen to 25% then 100%, and if it degrades, halt and roll back

choices.prompt: The module's walkthrough wires App Configuration using DefaultAzureCredential. According to the text, what does this specifically accomplish?
choices: No connection secret lives in code: it resolves a managed identity when running in Azure and the developer's credentials locally
It warms the deployment slot's caches before the swap so users never hit a cold start
It centralizes feature flags outside the code so they can target a percentage of users
It rewrites database schema changes to be additive so old and new versions never conflict
correct_answers: No connection secret lives in code: it resolves a managed identity when running in Azure and the developer's credentials locally

choices.prompt: Select the pitfalls the module explicitly warns about when running progressive deployments.
choices: Swapping a cold slot before the new instances are warmed up, which reintroduces the cold-start latency the slot was meant to avoid
Running a canary you cannot measure, which is just a slow full rollout because you never see the error rate and latency on the slice
Pushing all-at-once to 100% of production with no rollback plan, so every user hits a bad change and recovery requires a fresh deploy
Targeting a feature flag to a named group of users, which the module warns silently corrupts the swap routing
correct_answers: Swapping a cold slot before the new instances are warmed up, which reintroduces the cold-start latency the slot was meant to avoid
Running a canary you cannot measure, which is just a slow full rollout because you never see the error rate and latency on the slice
Pushing all-at-once to 100% of production with no rollback plan, so every user hits a bad change and recovery requires a fresh deploy

choices.prompt: The module says ring deployment 'suits changes where the risk is about real-world usage diversity rather than raw load.' Based on this framing, for which kind of change does the module say rings are the right fit?
choices: A change whose risk comes from the diversity of how real audiences actually use the product, promoted audience by audience to gain confidence
A change whose risk is purely a sudden spike in request volume, validated by load-testing a single percentage slice
A change that must cut over instantly and reversibly by flipping all traffic between two identical environments
A change to the database schema that must be made additive and deployed ahead of the code that requires it
correct_answers: A change whose risk comes from the diversity of how real audiences actually use the product, promoted audience by audience to gain confidence

llm.prompt: Larkspur must ship a high-risk change and wants both a small initial blast radius AND the ability to disable the new behavior in seconds without redeploying. Explain which two techniques combine to give them this, and what each one specifically contributes.
llm.reference_answer: The two techniques are canary (or ring) exposure plus a feature flag backed by Azure App Configuration's Feature Manager. Canary or ring exposure shrinks the initial blast radius: the change is released to only a small slice first (e.g. 5% of traffic, or ring 0 internal staff) so that if it degrades, only a fraction of users are affected before you halt and roll back. The feature flag decouples release from deploy: the new code ships to production disabled behind the flag, and you turn the new behavior on for a chosen audience without another deployment, then turn it off instantly if it misbehaves. The flag-off is a configuration change measured in seconds rather than a redeploy, which is what gives the instant kill-switch. Together, progressive exposure limits who is hit while the flag provides near-instant reversibility.

llm.prompt: Compare blue-green deployment with canary deployment for shrinking blast radius. Walk through the tradeoffs the module identifies for each, including their cost and what they require to work well.
llm.reference_answer: Both strategies answer how much of production sees the new version and how fast you can undo it, but with different tradeoffs. Blue-green runs two identical production environments; you deploy and warm the new version on the idle 'green', smoke-test it, then flip all traffic at once, which gives a near-instant rollback by flipping back to the still-running 'blue'. Its costs are running two full environments and an all-or-nothing cutover, so a bug that smoke tests miss still hits everyone, just reversibly. Canary instead releases to a small percentage of real traffic first (say 5%), widening to 25% then 100% only if it stays healthy, so the initial blast radius is much smaller. Canary trades blue-green's instant-flip simplicity for that smaller exposure and crucially requires good telemetry: you must watch error rate and latency on the canary slice and define in advance what 'unhealthy' means and what halts the rollout, otherwise it is just a slow full rollout.

llm.prompt: Walk through how Larkspur ships the rewritten route planner with two independent safety layers using a deployment slot and a feature flag, and explain the role of DefaultAzureCredential in the App Configuration wiring.
llm.reference_answer: Larkspur layers a slot swap with an App Configuration feature flag. The pipeline deploys the new build to a 'staging' slot (deployToSlotOrASE with slotName 'staging') rather than directly to production; warm-up happens in the slot, then an AzureAppServiceManage Swap Slots step exchanges the warmed instances into production as a routing change, so users never hit a cold start and a bad swap can be swapped back. The application reads the 'NewRoutePlanner' flag at runtime via IFeatureManager.IsEnabledAsync and only runs the new algorithm when the flag is on, so even after the code is live everywhere the new behavior stays off until Larkspur enables it for ring 0 (internal robots in the test warehouse), watches telemetry, then widens the percentage, and can kill it in seconds with no deployment. DefaultAzureCredential authenticates the App Configuration connection without any connection secret in code: it resolves a managed identity when running in Azure and the developer's credentials locally.
